questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup><mo>+</mo><mn>14</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>+</mo></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>yields</mi></mrow></msup><mo>⟶</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>Cr</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>33</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> millimole, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><msup><mi>Cr</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> millimole and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>067</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, then calculate the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of the solution.
questions.solution:
According to the Nernst's equation:

E = E°-RTnFlnQ

So, by given value of concentrations the above formula can be modified as

E = E°-0.059nFlogCr3+2Cr2O72-H+14

1.067 = 1.33-0.066×96500log15×10-324.5×10-3H+14

H+ = 10-2 M

Hence, the concentration of H+ ion will be

pH =-logH+pH=-log10-2=-(-2)×log1010pH = 2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Salts of $A, B$ and $C$ were electrolysed under identical conditions using the same quantity of electricity. It was found that when $2.1 \mathrm{~g}$ of $A$ was deposited, the weights of $B$ and $C$ deposited were $2.7 \mathrm{~g}$ and $9.6 \mathrm{~g}$. If the atomic mass of $A, B$ and $C$ respectively are 7,27 and 64 respectively, then the valancies of $A, B$ and $C$ respectively are
questions.solution:
Using concept of Faraday law,
Now, $A^{n_1^{+}}+n_1 e^{-} \longrightarrow A$
Similarly, $B^{n_2^{+}}+n_2 e^{-} \longrightarrow B$
and, $\mathrm{C}^{n_3^{+}}+n_3 e^{-} \longrightarrow C$
Since, charge is same then equivalent weight is same,
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & \frac{2.1}{\frac{7}{n_1}}=\frac{2.7}{\frac{27}{n_2}}=\frac{7.2}{\frac{48}{n_3}} \\
\therefore & \frac{3 \times n_1}{10}=\frac{1 \times n_2}{10}=\frac{3 \times n_3}{2 \times 10} \therefore n_1=\frac{n_2}{3}=\frac{n_3}{2} \\
\therefore & n_1=1, n_2=3 \text { and } n_3=2
\end{array}
$$
Hence, valencies of $A, B$ and $C$ are 1,3 and 2 .
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Find the emf of the following cell reaction, given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mrow><msup><mi>Cr</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><msup><mi>cr</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></mrow><mn>0</mn></msubsup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>72</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mrow><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>/</mo><mi>Fe</mi></mrow></msub><mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>0</mn></mmultiscripts></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>42</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cr</mi><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msup><mi>Cr</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>‖</mo><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></mfenced><mi>Fe</mi></math>
questions.solution:
The given cell is:

CrCr3+(0.1M)‖Fe2+(0.1M)Fe

The half reactions can be written as:

Cr→Cr3+ +3e-1         E° = -0.72VFe2++2e-1 → Fe       E° = -0.42V

The full reaction of the operating cell is:

3Fe2++2Cr → 2Cr3++3Fe

Now, emf of the cell reaction is 

E°cell =E°cathode-E°anodeE°cell=-0.42-(-0.72)= 0.30 V

Therefore,

Ecell = E°cell-nRTFlnQ

Ecell = E°cell - 2.303nRTFlog10Cr3+2Fe2+3Ecell = 0.3 - 0.0596log100.120.13Ecell ≈ 0.30 V
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the resistance of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution in a conductance cell is $300 \Omega$ and conductivity is $0.013 \mathrm{Scm}^{-1}$, then the value of cell constant is
questions.solution:
Conductivity $(\kappa)=\frac{1}{\text { Resistance }(R)} \times$ cell constant Cell constant $=\kappa \times R$
Given, $\kappa=0.0135 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \Rightarrow R=300 \Omega$
Cell constant $=0.0135 \times 300=3.9 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: When 10 A current is passed for 80 min, the volume of hydrogen gas liberated is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}^{+}+\underset{1 \mathrm{~F}}{e^{-}} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}$
At STP,
$\because 96500 \mathrm{C}$ liberates $=11.2 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}$
$\therefore 10 \times 80 \times 60 \mathrm{C}$ will liberate
$$
\begin{aligned} &=\frac{11.2 \times 10 \times 80 \times 60}{96500} \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2} \\=& 5.57 \mathrm{~L} \text { hydrogen gas } \end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>291</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, saturated solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> was found to have a specific conductivity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>648</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>ohm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and that of water being used is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>ohm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the ionic conductance's of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Ba</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>110</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>136</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>ohm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively, the solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BaSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>291</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> will be [Atomic masses of<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="" close="]"><mrow><mi>Ba</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>137</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>S</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>32</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
According to Kohlrausch's law

λ°mBaSO4 = λ°mBa2++λ°mSO42-λ°mBaSO4  = 110+136.6λ°mBaSO4  =  246.6 ohm-1cm-1

Now, the solubility product of BaSO4 can be determined by:

KBaSO4 = KBaSO4solution-KwaterKBaSO4 = 3.648×10-6-1.25×10-6KBaSO4 = 2.398×10-6 S cm-1

Finally, the molar conductivity will be:

λ°m = K×100Solubilityλ°m = 2.398×10-6×100246.6λ°m = 9.72×10-6×233λ°m = 2.26×10-3 gL
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passes through molten <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AlCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>608</mn></math> minutes. The mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Al</mi></math> deposited will be [Atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Al</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>]:-
questions.solution:
Given data :-

Current = 10 A.

Time = 1.608 min=96.48 s

Atomic mass of Al=27 g/mol

Oxidation state of Al=+3

Mass of aluminium deposited = Atomic mass ×time×currentF×valency factor

Mass of aluminium deposited = 27×96.48×1096480×3=0.09 g

Hence, the mass of Al deposited will be 0.09 g.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A \(200 \mathrm{~W}, 100 \mathrm{~V}\) bulb is connected in series with an electrolytic cell. If an aqueous solution of an Sn-salt is electrolysed for \(5 \mathrm{hrs}\), \(11.1 \mathrm{~g}\) of \(\mathrm{Sn}\) gets deposited. The chemical formula of the compound is (Given atomic weight of \(\mathrm{Sn}\) is \(118.7 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\)).
questions.solution:
\(\therefore w=\frac{E i t}{F}=\frac{\frac{A}{n} \times i \times t}{F}\)
\(\Rightarrow \quad n=\frac{A \times i \times t}{w \times F}=\frac{118.7 \times 2 \times(5 \times 3600)}{11.1 \times 96500}=3.989\)
\(A=\) atomic weight of \(\mathrm{Sn}=118.7 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\)
\(i=\text { current }=\frac{200 \mathrm{~W}}{100 \mathrm{~V}}=2 \mathrm{~A}\)
\(t=\text { time }=5 \mathrm{hrs}(5 \times 3600 \mathrm{~s})\)
\(E=\frac{A}{n}=\) equavalent weight of \(\mathrm{Sn}^{n+}\)
\(n=\) valency of \(\mathrm{Sn}\) in the salt
\(w=\) mass of \(\mathrm{Sn}\) deposited \(=11.1 \mathrm{~g}\)
Here, \(\mathrm{SnO}\) and \(\mathrm{SnO}_2\) are non-electrolytes (aqueous).
So, valency of \(\mathrm{Sn}\) will be 4 in \(\mathrm{SnCl}_4\left(\mathrm{Sn}^{4+}+4 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)\).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
If E ClO 3 - / ClO 4 - 0 = - 0 . 3 6  V  &  E ClO 3 - / ClO 2 - 0 = 0 . 3 3  V at 300 K.  The equilibrium concentration of perchlorate ion ClO 4 - which was initially 1.0 M in ClO 3 - when the reaction starts to attain the equilibrium,

2 ClO 3 - ⇌ ClO 2 - + ClO 4 -

Given :  Anti log(0.509) = 3.329
questions.solution:
The redox reaction can be split as

           Cl + 5 O 3 -1 ⟶ Cl + 7 O 4 -1 + 2 e OHR - anode Cl + 5 O 3 -1 + 2 e ⟶ Cl + 3 O 2 -1 RHR - cathode

     -------------------------------------------------------

       2 ClO 3 - 1 ⇄ n = 2e - ClO 4 - 1 + ClO 2 - 1

E cell o = E red  cath o - E red  anode o

        = 0.33 - 0.36 = -0.03 V

as  E red  Anode o = E ClO 4 - 1 / ClO 3 - 1 o = + 0 . 3 6  V

      = - E ClO 3 - 1 / ClO 4 - 1 o

at equilibrium Ecell = 0

E cell o = 0 . 0 5 9 n log  K eq = 0 . 0 5 9 2 log  K eq

Writing concentration of speces at equilibrium

2 ClO 3 - 1 ⇌ ClO 4 - 1 + ClO 2 - 1 1 - 2 x x x Molar Molar Molar

E cell o = - 0 . 0 3 = 0 . 0 5 9 2  log  x 2 1 - 2 x 2 = 0 . 0 5 9  log  x 1 - 2 x

- 0 . 0 3 0 . 0 5 9 = log x 1 - 2 x ⇒ log 1 - 2 x x = 0 . 0 3 0 . 0 5 9

log 1 - 2 x x = 0 . 5 0 9

1 - 2 x x = 3 . 2 2 9

1 - 2x = 3.229 x ⇒ 5.229x = 1

x = 1 5 . 2 2 9 = 0 . 1 9 1  M .
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The conductivity of a solution of concentration $0.1 \mathrm{~mol}$ $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$ of a weak monobasic acid (HA) (in $\mathrm{Scm}^{-1}$ ) is
(Given $\Lambda_{\mathrm{HA}}^{\circ}=400 \mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and degree dissociation
$(\alpha)$ of $\mathrm{HA}=0.02)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } \alpha=\frac{\Omega_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Omega_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}} \Rightarrow \Omega_{\mathrm{m}}=\alpha \times \Omega_{\mathrm{m}}^0 \\ & =0.02 \times 400=8.0 \mathrm{scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} . \\ & \text { Thus, } \mathrm{K}=\frac{\Omega_{\mathrm{m}} \times \mathrm{C}}{1000}=\frac{8.0 \times 0.1}{1000}=8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \text {. }\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Salts of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> (atomic weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math>), <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> (atomic weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn></math>) and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> (atomic weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math>) were electrolysed under identical conditions using the same quantity of electricity. It was found that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> was deposited, the weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> deposited are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> respectively. The valences of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are, respectively,
questions.solution:
According to the Faraday's law of electrolysis,

W=ZQ=E96500Q

For the compound A, 

Equivalent mass of A = Atomic massValency=8x

x is the valency factor for compound A.

So, W=E96500Q=8x96500Qx=72.4×96500Q

For compound B,

Equivalent mass of B=Atomic massValency=18y

y is the valency factor for compound B.

So, W=E96500Q=18y96500Qy=271.8×96500Q

For compound C,

Equivalent mass of C = Atomic massValency=50z

z is the valency factor for compound C.

So, W=E96500Q=50z96500Qz=507.5×96500Q

Hence, x:y:z=82.4 : 181.8 : 507.5=3.33 : 10 : 6.66x:y:z = 1:3:2

Hence, the valencies of A,B and C respectively are 1,3,2.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which of the following curve will represent the variation of conductance C during titration of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> by gradual addition of NaOH solution (V)
questions.solution: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> is weak acid, NaOH is strong base neutralisation produces strong electrolyte <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> . Thus, conductance C is low in the beginning, increases gradually and slowly till equivalence point and thereafter increases rapidly.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In the electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride with inert electrodes the products obtained at anode and cathode respectively are
questions.solution:
In the electrolysis of aqueous sodium chloride with inert electrodes, the products obtained at anode and cathode respectively are Cl2 and H2.
The solution contains four ions Na+,Cl-,H+,OH.
Following reaction takes place:
At cathode
Na+aq+e-→Nas;                              ENa+/Na-=-2.71V2H2Ol+2e-→H2g+2O-Haq;                                        EH2O/H2o=-0.83V
At anode
2Cl-aq→Cl2g+2e-;ECl-/Cl2o=-1.36V2H2O2l→Og+4H+aq+4e-;                                                                  EH2O/O2o=-1.23 V
EH2O/H2o>ENa+/Nao. is formed at cathode.
Whereas EH2O/O2o>ECl-/Cl2o. So, formed at anode.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A lead storage battery is discharged. During the charging of this battery, the reaction that occurs at anode, is
questions.solution:
During recharging, the cell is operated like an electrolytic cell. The electrode reactions are the reverse of those that occur during discharge.
At cathod
$$
\mathrm{PbSO}_4(s)+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Pb}(s)+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})
$$

At anode $\mathrm{PbSO}_4(s)+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow$
$$
\mathrm{PbO}_2(s)+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 e^{-}
$$
Net reaction
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{PbSO}_4(s)+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l) \longrightarrow \\
& \mathrm{Pb}(s)+\mathrm{PbO}_2(s)+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the molar conductances at infinite dilution for the strong electrolytes $\mathrm{NaOH}, \mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ are $248 \times 10^{-4}, 126 \times 10^{-4}$ and $280 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Sm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively, $\lambda_m^{\circ} \mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ in $\mathrm{Sm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{BaCl}_2+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2+2 \mathrm{NaCl} \\ & \lambda_{m \mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2}^{\infty}=\lambda_{m \mathrm{BaCl}_2}^{\infty}+2 \lambda_{m \mathrm{NaOH}}^{\infty}-2 \lambda_{m \mathrm{NaCl}}^{\infty}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =280 \times 10^{-4}+2 \times 248 \times 10^{-4} \\ & \quad-2 \times 126 \times 10^{-4} \\ & =(280+496-252) \times 10^{-4} \\ & =524 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Sm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ cell is $1 \cdot 049 \mathrm{~V}$ and involves transfer of 2 electrons, calculate equilibrium constant of cell?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{0}=1.049 \mathrm{~V}, \quad \mathrm{n}=2, \quad \mathrm{~K}=?$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{0}=\frac{0.0592}{\mathrm{n}} \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}$
$\therefore \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{0} \times \mathrm{n}}{0.0592}=\frac{1.049 \times 2}{0.0592}$
$\therefore \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}=35.439$
$\therefore \mathrm{K} =$ Antilog $(35.439)$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}=2.75 \times 10^{35}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is the time (in sec) required for depositing all the silver present in $125 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgNO}{ }_3$ solution by passing a current of $241.25 \mathrm{~A}$ ? (1F $=96500$ coulombs $)$
questions.solution:
Given $125 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution. It means that
$\because 1000 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution contains 
$=108 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Ag}$
$\therefore 125 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution contains
$=\frac{108 \times 125}{1000} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Ag}$
$=13.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Ag}$
$\because 108 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}$ is deposited by $96500 \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore 13.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}$ is deposited by $=\frac{96500}{108} \times 13.5$
$=12062.5 \mathrm{C}$
$Q=i t$
$t=\frac{Q}{i}=\frac{12062.5}{241.25}=50$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

Potential of hydrogen electrode at $\mathrm{pH}=10$ is $-0.591 \mathrm{~V}$.
Reduction potential of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}$ is $0.36 \mathrm{~V}$.
Oxidation potential of $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ is $0.76 \mathrm{~V}$.
$\frac{2.303 R T}{F}=\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96500}=0.059$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When the same quantity of electricity is passed through the aqueous solutions of the given electrolytes for the same amount of time, which metal will be deposited in maximum amount on the cathode?
questions.solution:
By Faraday's law of electrolysis:

Equal amount of charge causes deposition of equal number of equivalents of different metals. eg. 1F charge  will deposit one equivalent of any metal.

Discharged mass of one equivalent of Zn from ZnCl2= 65.42=32.7 g

Discharged mass of one equivalent of Fe from FeCl3=563=18.67 g

Discharged mass of one equivalent of Ag from AgNO3=1081=108 g

Discharged mass of one equivalent of Ni from NiCl2=58.72=29.35 g 

So, Silver metal will be deposited in maximum amount on the cathode.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Given the standard potential for the following half-cell reaction at 298 K,

Cu+aq+e-→Cus; E°=0.52 V

Cu2+aq+e-→Cu+aq; E°=0.16 V

Calculate the ΔG° (kJ) for the reaction, 2Cu+aq→Cus+Cu2+
questions.solution:
Cu++e-→Cu;E°=0.52 V

ΔG1=-nFE°=-1×96500×0.52 ...(i)

Cu2++e-→Cu+;E°=0.16 V

or Cu+→Cu2++e-;E°=-0.16 V

ΔG2=-1×96500×-0.16 ...(ii)

On adding equations (i) and (ii), we get

2Cu+→Cu+Cu2+ ,

ΔG=ΔG1+ΔG2

=-96500×0.52+96500×0.16

=96500-0.52+0.16

=-96500×0.36

= −34740 J = −34.740 kJ
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Consider the following electrode processes of a cell,
$$
\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2+e^{-} \quad\left[\mathrm{MCl}+e^{-} \rightarrow M+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]
$$
If EMF of this cell is $-1.140 \mathrm{~V}$ and $E^{\circ}$ value of the cell is $-0.55 \mathrm{~V}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$, the value of the equilibrium constant of the sparingly soluble salt $M \mathrm{Cl}$ is in the order of
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{MCl}+e^{-} \longrightarrow M+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$cathode (reduction) $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2+e^{-}$anode (oxidation)
$$
\mathrm{MCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{M}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2
$$
The $K_C$ of the cell reaction is calculated from
Nernst equation $E_{\text {cell }}=E^{\circ}$ cell $-\frac{0.059}{n} \log K_c$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& -1.140=-0.55-\frac{0.059}{1} \log c \\
& -0.59=-0.059 \log K_c \\
& \log K_c=\frac{0.59}{0.059}=10 \\
& \therefore \quad K_C=10^{10} \\
& K_{s p} \text { is for } M+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow M \mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow M^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
& \therefore K_{s p}=\frac{1}{K_c} \\
& \quad=\frac{1}{10^{10}}=10^{-10}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The conductivity of a solution containing $2.08 \mathrm{~g}$ of anhydrous barium chloride in $200 \mathrm{~mL}$ solution is $6 \times 10^{-3}$ $\mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. The molar conductivity of the solution (in $\left.\mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$ is $\underline{x} \times 10^2$. The value of $\underline{x}$ is
(Atomic mass of $\mathrm{Ba}=137, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5$ )
questions.solution:
Conductivity (k) $=6 \times 10^{-3} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$
$$
\mathrm{V}=200 \mathrm{~mL}=0.2 \mathrm{~L}
$$
mass $=2.08 \mathrm{~g}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { molar mass }}\right)}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\left(\frac{2.08}{208}\right)}{0.2} \\
& =0.05 \mathrm{M} .
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{K} \times 1000}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\left(6 \times 10^{-3}\right)(1000)}{0.05} \\
& =120=1.20 \times 10^2
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, $\mathrm{x}=1.2$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A saturated solution of $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ with agar-agar is used to make a 'salt-bridge' because
options: size of $\mathrm{K}^{+}$is greater than that of $\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$
velocity of $\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$is greater than that of $\mathrm{K}^{+}$
Both velocities and sizes of $\mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$are nearly the same
questions.solution: $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ is used in salt bridge because velocity of $\mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$ions is same due to which ions do not accumulate.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The molar conductivity of $0.027 \mathrm{M}$ methanoic acid is $40.42 \mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The value of dissociation constant of this acid is
$\left(\right.$ Given $\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^{\circ}=349.6 \mathrm{~S}_{.}, \mathrm{cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}}^{\circ}=54.6 \mathrm{~S}$ $\left.\mathrm{cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{o}}(\mathrm{HCOOH})=\lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^0+\lambda_{\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}}^0 \\ & =349.6+54.6 \\ & =404.2 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0}=\frac{40.42}{404.2}=0.1 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}=\frac{0.027(0.1)^2}{1-0.1} \\ & =0.0003=3.0 \times 10^{-4}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Copper is to be electrodeposited on a nickel block of $(20 \times 5) \mathrm{cm}^2$ area by using $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$ as electrolyte. How much quantity of electricity is needed to deposit a $3.6 \mu \mathrm{m}$ layer of copper? [Atomic weight of $\mathrm{Cu}=63.5 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ Density of $\left.\mathrm{CuSO}_4=8.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}\right]$
questions.solution:
$\therefore W=\frac{E Q}{F}=\frac{\frac{A}{n} \times Q}{F}$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow \quad A^{\prime} & \times h \times d=\frac{A \times Q}{n F} \\
\Rightarrow \quad Q & =\frac{\left(A^{\prime} \times h\right) \times d \times n \times F}{A} \\
& =\frac{0.036 \times 8.9 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5}=973.814 \mathrm{C} \simeq 974 \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$
$[\because$ Volume of Cu-deposited on the nickel block $=$ area of the block $\times$ thickness of copper layer $=A^{\prime} \times h=(20 \times 5) \times\left(3.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^2\right) \mathrm{cm}^3$
$=0.036 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \quad\left[\because 1 \mu \mathrm{m}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}=10^{-2} \times 10^2 \mathrm{~cm}\right]$
Where, $d=$ density of $\mathrm{Cu}=8.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$
$\begin{aligned}
& n=2 \\
& F=96500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \\
& A=\text { atomic weight of } \mathrm{Cu}^*=63.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \text { lata informations, } \\
& \text { density of CuSO } \left.\mathrm{Cu}_4^{2+}+2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu} \downarrow\right] \\
& \text { rong : it will be density of } \mathrm{Cu}=8.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}]
\end{aligned}$
[* In data informations,
Given, density of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4=8.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$
If is wrong : it will be density of $\mathrm{Cu}=8.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$ ]
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
The electrode potential for
$$
\begin{aligned}
& M^{2+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow M^{+}(a q) \\
& M^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow M(s)
\end{aligned}
$$
are $+0.15 \mathrm{~V}$ and $+0.50 \mathrm{~V}$ respectively. The value of $E^{\circ}{ }_{M^{2+} / M}$ will be
questions.solution:
Given,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& M^{2+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow M^{+}(a q), E_1^{\circ}=+0.15 \mathrm{~V} \\
& M^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \longrightarrow M(s), E_2^{\circ}=0.5 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\because$ Free energy, i.e. $\left[\Delta G^{\circ}\right]=-n F E^{\circ}$
where, $n=$ number of electron involved
$$
E^{\circ}=\text { standard electrode potential. }
$$
$F=$ Faraday's constant
To find $E_3^{\circ}$ for $E_{\left(M^{2+} / M\right)}^{\circ}$
Thus,
$$
\begin{array}{lr}
-\Delta G_1^{\circ}=n E_1^{\circ}=E_1^{\circ} & (\because n=1) \\
-\Delta G_2^{\circ}=n E_2^{\circ}=E_{{ }_2}{ }_2 & (\because n=1) \\
-\Delta G_3^{\circ}=n E_3=2 E_3^{\circ} & (\because n=2) \\
\text { [For reaction : } M^{2+}(a q)+2 e^{-} & \longrightarrow M(s) \text { ] }
\end{array}
$$

Therefore,
$$
\begin{aligned}
-\Delta G_3^{\circ} & =(-) \Delta G_1^{\circ}+(-) \Delta G_2^{\circ} \\
-2 E_3^{\circ} & =-\left(n E_1^{\circ}+n E_2^{\circ}\right) \\
2 E_3^{\circ} & =E_1^{\circ}+E_2^{\circ} \\
2 E_3^{\circ} & =0.15+0.5 \\
2 E_3^{\circ} & =0.65 \mathrm{~V} \\
E_3^{\circ} & =\frac{0.65}{2}=0.325 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, option (c) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which one of the following has the highest molar conductivity?
questions.solution:
The molar conductivity of an electrolyte
solution is defined as its conductivity ( $\mathrm{k}$ ) divide by its molar concentration (C). i.c.,
Molar conductivity $(\lambda)=\frac{\kappa}{C}$
The measurement of molar conductivity of solutions of coordinate compounds helps to estimate the number of ions furnished by compound in the solution.
By comparing molar conductivity we are able to predict the number of ions present in the solution. More be the number of ions, more is the value of molar conductivity.
$\because$ Potassium hexacynoferrate (II), give maximum number of ions.
Thus, (d) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
For the following cell reaction,
$\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{Ag}\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right| \mathrm{AgCl}\left|\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}\right| \mathrm{Cl}_2, \mathrm{Pt} \\
\Delta G_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{AgCl})=-109 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\
\Delta G_f^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}\right)=-129 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\
\Delta G_f^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right)=78 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{gathered}$
$E^{\circ}$ of the cell is
questions.solution:
For the given cell, $\mathrm{Ag}\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right| \mathrm{AgCl}\left|\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}\right| \mathrm{Cl}_2, \mathrm{Pt}$ the cell reactions are as follows
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \Delta G_{\text {reaction }}^{\circ} & =\Sigma \Delta G_p^{\circ}-\Sigma \Delta G_R^{\circ} \\ & =(78-129)-(-109) \\ & =+58 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ \Delta G^{\circ} & =-n F E^{\circ} \\ 58 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} & =-1 \times 96500 \times E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} \\ E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} & =\frac{-58 \times 1000}{96500} \\ & =-0.6 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Which is the correct equation for the reaction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>OH</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
questions.solution:
Reaction of AgCl with NH4OH is a complexation reaction.

AgCl+2NH4OH→AgNH32++Cl-+2H2O

AgCl is white colour water insoluble compound but in the presence of NH3 its solubility increase due to formation of complex Ag(NH3)2+.

[Ag(NH3)2]Cl is the formed complex.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Calculate ΔGo for the following cell reaction
Zn s + Ag 2 O s + H 2 O l → Zn 2+ aq +2Ag s +2 OH − aq

EAg+/Ag0=+0.80V and EZn+2/Zn0=-0.76V ,F=96500

(Given:  K sp of AgOH = 2×  10 −8 )
questions.solution:
Calculation for EOH-Ag2OAg-
Reduction [2Ag++2e-→2Ags,∆G1o=-2×F×EoAg+|Ag
Ionisation [Ag2O+H2Ol→2Ag++2OH-∆G2o=-2.303RTlogksp2
Net Ag2O+H2Ol+2e-→ 2Ags+2OH-,∆G3o=-2×F×EOH-Ag2OAgo
∆G3o=∆G1o+∆G2o
+2×F×EOH-Ag2OAgo=+2×F×EoAg+|Ag+2.303RTlogksp22F
EOH-Ag2OAgo=EoAg+|Ag+0.062×2log⁡ksp
=0.8+0.06log⁡2×10-8
=0.8+0.06-8+0.3
=0.8+0.06×-7.7
=0.8-0.462
=0.338Volt≅0.34V ∆Go=-nFEceuo
=-nFEco-EARed
=-2×EOH-Ag2OAgo-EZn+2|Zno
=-2×96500×0.34+0.76
=-2×96500×1.1volt
=212300J
=212.3 kJ
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For the oxidation of $0.2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{FeSO}_4$ solution 0.965 amperes current is passed through it for 1 hour. The volume of the solution that is oxidised in $\mathrm{mL}$ is
questions.solution:
The equation is
$$
\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}
$$
1 mole is deposited by $2 \times 96500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=193000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
The charged supplied $=0.965 \times 60 \times 60$
$$
=3474 \mathrm{C} \quad(\because Q=i \times t)
$$
Number of moles deposited by this charge
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{3474}{193000}=0.018 \mathrm{~mol} \\
& \text { Molarity }=\frac{\text { Number of moles } \times 1000}{\text { Volume in } \mathrm{mL}} \\
& \text { Volume }(\text { in } \mathrm{mL})=\frac{\text { Number of moles } \times 1000}{M(\text { molarity })} \\
& =\frac{0.018 \times 1000}{0.2}=90 \mathrm{~mL} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: For the oxidation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>FeSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>965</mn></math> amperes current is passed through it for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> hour. The volume of the solution that is oxidised in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mL</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Given that,

0.2 M FeSO4 and current = 0.965 amp-hr=0.965×3600 coulomb

Dissociation of FeSO4=Fe2++SO42-

n-factor = 2

We know that, 

Normality = Molarity ×n-factor

⇒Normality = 2×0.2=0.4 N

Here, 96500 coulomb = 1 eq. Fe2+.

⇒0.965×3600 coulomb = 0.965×360096500 eq. of Fe2+ = 0.036 eq.

Also, Normality =No. of gram eq.Volume(L) ⇒0.4=0.036Vol.(L)⇒Vol.(L)=0.0360.4=0.09 L =90 mL

Hence, the volume of the solution that is oxidised in mL=90 mL
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If ' $A$ ' is the reactant and ' $P$ ' is the product, which one of the following is the correct form of Nernst equation?
questions.solution:
According to Nernst equation :
$\begin{aligned} & E=E^{\circ}-\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{[\text { Product }]}{[\text { Reactant }]} \\ & \text { or, } \quad E=E^{\circ}-\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \frac{[P]}{[A]} \\ & \text { or, } \quad E-E^{\circ}=-\frac{R T}{n F} \ln \left[\frac{P}{A}\right] \\ & \text { or, } \quad\left[\frac{A}{P}\right]=e^{\left[\frac{n F}{R T}\left(E-E^{\circ}\right)\right]} \\ & \text { or } \quad \frac{[A]}{[P]}=\exp ^{\left[\frac{n F}{R T}\left(E-E^{\circ}\right)\right]} \\ & \end{aligned}$
Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Find the observed EMF of the cell \(\mathrm{Cd}\left|\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right| \mathrm{Cd}\) under conditions, internal resistance of \(4 \Omega\) and producing a current of \(0.15 \mathrm{~A}\).
(Given, \(E^{\circ} \mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}=0.35 \mathrm{~V}\)
and \(\quad E_{\mathrm{Cd}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cd}}^{\circ}=-0.4 \mathrm{~V}\) )
questions.solution:
The cell representation is
\(\mathrm{Cd} / \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \mid \mathrm{Cu}\)
Note In the statement of the question, this portion is written as
\({ }^" \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \mid \mathrm{Cd}\) " which is wrong.
The cell reaction is
\(\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{Cd}(s)+\underset{(0.01 \mathrm{M})}{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}} \longrightarrow \underset{(0.01 \mathrm{M})}{\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}}+\mathrm{Cu}(s) \\
Q=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}\right][\mathrm{Cu}]}{[\mathrm{Cd}]\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}=\frac{0.01 \times 1}{1 \times 0.01}=1
\end{gathered}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
E_{\text {cell }} & =\left(E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}-E_{\mathrm{Cd}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cd}}^{\circ}\right)-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log Q \\
& =[0.35-(-0.40)]-0 \quad[\because \log Q=\log \mathrm{l}=0] \\
& =0.75 \mathrm{~V} \\
\Rightarrow E_{\text {cell }}^{\text {obscrved }} & =E_{\text {intemal }}-E_{\text {extemal }} \\
& =0.75-(\text { resistance } \times \text { current }) \\
& =0.75-(4 \times 0.15)=(0.75-0.60) \mathrm{V} \\
& =0.15 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}\)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Match the entries from Column I and Column II and choose the correct order.
questions.solution:
The correct matching between Column I and Column II is
(A) Leclanche cell-anode reaction :
\(\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 e^{-}\)
Cathode :
\(\mathrm{MnO}_2+\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnO}(\mathrm{OH})+\mathrm{NH}_3\)
(B) Fuel cell : \(2 \mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\) Energy (gets converted into electrical energy).
(C) Ni-Cd cell : Rechargeable (secondary) cell. Hence, option (c) is correct.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
If the \(E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}\) of an equilibrium reaction \(A(s)+2 B^{2+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+2 B(s)\)
at \(298 \mathrm{~K}\) is \(0.59 \mathrm{~V}\), the equilibrium constant \(K_c\) is
questions.solution:
For the reaction,
\(\begin{gathered}
A+2 B^{2+}(a q) \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}(a q)+2 B(s) \\
\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K_c=-n F E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} \quad \ldots (i)
\end{gathered}\)
where,
\(\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ} & =\text { Gibbs free energy } \\
n & =\text { number of electrons involved }(=2) \\
F & =\text { Faraday's constant }=96500 \mathrm{C} \text { charge. } \\
E^{\circ} & =\text { standard electrode cell potential }(=0.59 \mathrm{~V}) \\
R & =\text { gas constant } \\
T & =\text { temperature }(=298 \mathrm{~K})
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore\) From Eq. (i), we have
\(\begin{aligned}
\text {or } \log K_c & =\frac{n F E^{\circ}}{2.303 R T} \\
& =\frac{2 \times 96500 \times 0.59}{2303 \times 8.314 \times 298}=-19.96 \approx 20 \\
\therefore \quad K_c & =1.0 \times 10^{20}
\end{aligned}\)
Hence, option (c) is the correct answer.
Alternatively
At, \(T=298 \mathrm{~K}\)
\(\ln \quad K_c=\frac{n E_{\text {ccll }}^{\circ}}{0.059}=\frac{2 \times 0.59}{0.059}=20\)
\(\therefore \quad K_c=1 \times 10^{20}\)
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When electric current is passed through acidified water for $1930 \mathrm{~s}, 1120 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas is collected (at STP) at the cathode. What is the current passed in amperes?
questions.solution:
Electrolysis of water takes place as follows
$$
\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\text { cathode }}{\mathrm{H}^{+}}+\underset{\text { anode }}{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}
$$
At anode
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{OH}^{-} \stackrel{\text { Oxidation }}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{OH}+e^{-} \\
& 4 \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$
At cathode
$$
2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 e^{-} \stackrel{\text { Reduction }}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{H}_2
$$
Given, time, $\quad t=1930 \mathrm{~s}$
Number of moles of hydrogen collected
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1120 \times 10^{-3}}{22.4} \text { moles } \\
& =0.05 \mathrm{moles}
\end{aligned}
$$
$\because 1$ mole of hydrogen is deposited by $=2$
moles of electrons
$\therefore 0.05$ moles of hydrogen will be deposited by
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =2 \times 0.05 \\
& =0.10 \text { mole of electrons }
\end{aligned}
$$
Charge,
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q & =n F \\
& =0.1 \times 96500
\end{aligned}
$$
Charge, $Q=i t$
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.1 \times 96500 & =i \times 1930 \\
i & =\frac{0.1 \times 96500}{1930} \\
& =5.0 \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
